title: Texas firm tops Tesla with innovative solar rooftop solution - Interesting Engineering
description: Using LFP batteries, Yotta Energy has eliminated the need to install a separate battery pack and the extra wiring that it needs.
bodyText: Yotta Energy, a clean energy firm based in Austin, Texas, has developed an innovative rooftop solution that tops Tesla’s in this domain. The solution isn’t about what Yotta has added to the solar energy setup but what it has managed to remove or replace from the mix: A battery pack that is separately installed. When Tesla began offering its innovative solar tiles, it also offered an energy storage solution called the Powerwall. The sleek-looking battery was a major improvement over the previous car-like battery solutions. Tesla leveraged the availability of the lithium-ion battery to make its solution far superior to others in the market and has crossed over 500,000 installations. Although Tesla’s battery looked sleeker, it still needed separate wiring and dedicated space for installation. With further improvements in battery technology, Yotta Energy can now offer an energy storage solution that makes all this seem like extra work and unnecessary hassle. Yotta’s strategy is to stick the battery pack behind the solar panels. Once again, the key ingredient in this offering is advancement in technology. Instead of lithium-ion batteries, Yotta is working with lithium-iron-phosphate (LFP) chemistry for its batteries. These batteries are cheaper to make because they use less expensive components. At the same time, they are also heat stable, allowing for the battery pack to be installed outdoors. Yotta took the acid test when it placed its batteries through Texas summer, where temperatures hovered over 100 degrees Fahrenheit (37 degrees Celsius) for multiple days. This year, the company is testing them in far northern US, where temperatures dip to freezing points. LFP batteries also work at low temperatures, but the company isn’t taking any chances. Yotta’s chances of success aren’t just backed by chemistry alone. The company has gone to great lengths to ensure that its battery pack works well through the rigors of rooftop installations. To begin with, the battery pack is painted white and reflects all the energy it can. The battery design also includes passive cooling fins that dissipate heat when needed. Inside the battery pack, the LFP cells are mounted in the center with two ceramic layers that work as insulators to keep the heat and cold outside and away from the cells. The cells are also covered with a layer of wax, which stays solid at optimal temperatures. If temperatures rise beyond a certain point, the excess heat will be used to change the wax’s state, reducing the battery’s risks. Direct current generated by solar cells must be converted into alternating current before it can be stored in batteries. In conventional energy storage solutions, an inverter does this near the battery pack. Since Yotta’s solutions do not have a central battery pack, the company developed microinverters that carry out the process near the cells and quickly add it to the battery packs. Yotta’s 1 kilowatt hour (kWh) battery pack weighs just 53 pounds (24 kgs). It can be modularly placed behind the solar panels to add up to 1.5 megawatts (MW) of storage capacity. The batteries also serve as additional weight on the solar racks where the photovoltaic modules are placed, eliminating the need to add ballast weights to the installation. The solution might only be suited for smaller installations now, but that is the growing and underserved market. Yotta is at the right place at the right time. Ameya is a science writer based in Hyderabad, India. A Molecular Biologist at heart, he traded the micropipette to write about science during the pandemic and does not want to go back. He likes to write about genetics, microbes, technology, and public policy.
